contextual_chunks.title: The Gaelic Athlete, vol. VIII, no. 192, Saturday September 4th 1915
contextual_chunks.text:
September 4, 1915

Ireland's Record in Athletic Sports

From the Tailtean Games to the Present Time

Including the Complete History of the Rise and Development of the G.A.A.

By "VIGILANT."

XXX - A FIERCE BLIZZARD

The Freeman's Journal now continued to make trouble for the Association in general, and the Executive in particular with increasing zeal. The GAA, which had been the only one in the country to have a handicapped over the question of the handicap, was entirely defeated by the Freeman's Freeman's Journal Athletic Club and its in- sistance on its "right" to ignore a rule of the Association that had been confirmed at the Thirtieth Congress of the G.A.A. was regarded as a defeat for the G.A.A., and made some scathing references to the Executive's power of suspension. This, however, was only a small step in the direction of the Freeman's Journal Athletic Club, and left nothing undone to discredit the Executive. The committee in charge of the clause of the G.A.A. had taken a different view of the situation, and upheld the rules of the Association and the decisions of the Executive by debarring sus- pended athletes from the G.A.A. The Freeman's Journal looked forward to the ple

contextual_chunks.title: The Gaelic Athlete, vol. VIII, no. 191, Saturday August 28th 1915
contextual_chunks.text:
August 28, 1915

Ireland's Record in Athletic Sports

From the Tailtean Games to the Present Time

Including the Complete History of the Rise and Development of the G.A.A.

By "VIGILANT."

XXIX.-STILL IN THE STORMS

The Executive was meeting trouble in every quarter. In some places it arose out of differences with county committees over the appointment of handicappers, or, perhaps, the constitution. Over in Ballina a local sports committee came into contact with the Executive over the appointment of a handicapper with the result that the Executive "proclaimed" the meeting. The sports committee, however, were determined on holding the meeting, and transferred their allegiance to the Irish Amateur Athletic Association. What had just occurred in the West of Ireland was repeated in Dublin almost at the same time.

The G.A.A. was on the handicapper of their own choice, Mr. P. P. Sutton, in opposition to the wishes of the Executive which naturally desired that the services of the official handicapper, Mr. F. B. Dinneen, should be available at all such sports meetings. This difference gave rise to a very sharp cleavage of opinion in Dublin.

The G.A.A. found itself in the Dublin County Co

contextual_chunks.title: The Gaelic Athlete, vol. VIII, no. 185, Saturday July 17th 1915
contextual_chunks.text:
July 17, 1815

Ireland's Record in Athletic Sports

From the Tailtean Games to the Present Time

Including the Complete History of the Rise and Development of the G.A.A.

By "VIGILANT."

XXII. - The G.A.A. in 1886 (continued).

The second annual athletic championship meeting held by the Gaelic Athletic Association took place at Ballinlough, Dublin, on September 11th, 1886, and produced some remarkably good performances. Mitchell made new records with the 60 lb. weight and the 16 lb. hammer.

The second annual convention which was to be held on the anniversary of the founding of the Association, November 1st, 1886, was not held until a fortnight after that date had elapsed. There was a big attendance of delegates present. Mr. Maurice Davin presided, and some very useful legislation for the better development of the games was accomplished. Wrestling in football matches was abolished. The formation of county committees was approved. The constitution of the Association was in many respects revised and many emendations were made to the hurling and football rules. The officials were elected as follows: President, Mr. Maurice Davin; Vice-Presidents, Messrs. J. K. Bracken, Templemore; J. E

contextual_chunks.text:
June 26, 1915

Ireland's Record in Athletic Sports

From the Tailtean Games to the Present Time

Including the Complete History of the Rise and Development of the G.A.A.

By "VIGILANT."

XIX.-The War of 1855 (continued).

WHILE the progress of the Gaelic athletic association in Ireland was attracting the attention of the British press, the public opinion of the country was going to be influenced by the Irish Nationalist's idea of a new system of sport. The Gaels, however, had no inclination to criticise the state of things that permitted athletic sports and football matches to be made a business calling and a means of their gambling on a wholesale scale throughout England. On the contrary, the correct thing for Ireland to do, according to her self-constituted public instructors, was to go on imitating England's idea of sport as had been the case up to 1884. At the very time when this pharosical fault was so energetically directed against Ireland's National Athletic Association that was to be the first in the world, the Gaels were almost entirely dependent upon the public opinion of the country. In 1888 the football players raised a terrible clamour for payment and the cry increased
